verses.text: In the days of the judges, famine drives a Bethlehem family into Moab — and there Elimelech and both his sons die. Naomi is left with two foreign daughters-in-law and no husband, no sons, no bread. The book opens in loss; that is so often the soil in which God plants His quiet redemptions.

In the days of the judges, famine drives a Bethlehem family into Moab — and there Elimelech and both his sons die. Naomi is left with two foreign daughters-in-law and no husband, no sons, no bread. The book opens in loss; that is so often the soil in which God plants His quiet redemptions.

In the days of the judges, famine drives a Bethlehem family into Moab — and there Elimelech and both his sons die. Naomi is left with two foreign daughters-in-law and no husband, no sons, no bread. The book opens in loss; that is so often the soil in which God plants His quiet redemptions.

In the days of the judges, famine drives a Bethlehem family into Moab — and there Elimelech and both his sons die. Naomi is left with two foreign daughters-in-law and no husband, no sons, no bread. The book opens in loss; that is so often the soil in which God plants His quiet redemptions.

Hearing that the LORD has visited His people with bread, Naomi turns homeward. The first step out of Moab is the first step back toward God. Even a heart heavy with grief can move in the right direction once it hears that the LORD has remembered His own.

Hearing that the LORD has visited His people with bread, Naomi turns homeward. The first step out of Moab is the first step back toward God. Even a heart heavy with grief can move in the right direction once it hears that the LORD has remembered His own.

Naomi urges her daughters-in-law to return to their mothers' homes, praying, 'The LORD deal kindly with you, as ye have dealt with the dead and with me.' Even in her sorrow she seeks their good and commends them to the covenant kindness of God.

Naomi urges her daughters-in-law to return to their mothers' homes, praying, 'The LORD deal kindly with you, as ye have dealt with the dead and with me.' Even in her sorrow she seeks their good and commends them to the covenant kindness of God.

Naomi urges her daughters-in-law to return to their mothers' homes, praying, 'The LORD deal kindly with you, as ye have dealt with the dead and with me.' Even in her sorrow she seeks their good and commends them to the covenant kindness of God.

She presses them harder, sure she has nothing left to give and that 'the hand of the LORD is gone out against me.' Grief can misread God's dealings as His enmity. Naomi feels abandoned at the very hour He is preparing her greatest mercy.

She presses them harder, sure she has nothing left to give and that 'the hand of the LORD is gone out against me.' Grief can misread God's dealings as His enmity. Naomi feels abandoned at the very hour He is preparing her greatest mercy.

She presses them harder, sure she has nothing left to give and that 'the hand of the LORD is gone out against me.' Grief can misread God's dealings as His enmity. Naomi feels abandoned at the very hour He is preparing her greatest mercy.

Orpah kisses Naomi and turns back; Ruth clings. 'Whither thou goest, I will go... thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God.' A Moabite woman binds herself to Naomi's God for life and death. This is true conversion — leaving all behind to take refuge in the LORD.

Orpah kisses Naomi and turns back; Ruth clings. 'Whither thou goest, I will go... thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God.' A Moabite woman binds herself to Naomi's God for life and death. This is true conversion — leaving all behind to take refuge in the LORD.

Orpah kisses Naomi and turns back; Ruth clings. 'Whither thou goest, I will go... thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God.' A Moabite woman binds herself to Naomi's God for life and death. This is true conversion — leaving all behind to take refuge in the LORD.

Naomi comes home so changed the town hardly knows her. 'Call me Mara,' she says — bitter, for she went out full and came back empty. Yet she returns 'in the beginning of barley harvest.' Even as she names her emptiness, God has quietly set the table for her restoration.
